---
title: "Distance Does Not Explain Price"
subtitle: "How airline market power shapes what you pay"
author: "Akshay Arun, Alexa Nakanishi, & Norman Wang"
format:
  html:
    theme: yeti
    toc: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5
citeproc: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

Have you ever felt like airline ticket prices have surged after you start researching flights? Do flight fares and the distance you are flying ever not make logical sense? 


![](images/jetblue-tweet.png){fig-cap="Deleted JetBlue Tweet. Source: https://x.com/CultureCrave/status/2046631236941295920"}

In a now deleted tweet, JetBlue has, it seems, admitted to surveillance pricing. Surveillance pricing, also known as “personalized algorithmic pricing”, is a tactic used by companies where prices change dynamically based on the data of individual consumers. [@barton_surveillance_pricing] While federal-level investigations by the Federal Trade Commission into surveillance pricing have approached a standstill, many states have begun cracking down on it. In fact, Maryland is on track to become the first state to ban surveillance pricing for groceries. [@npr_surveillance_pricing_maryland] Yet, surveillance pricing may be true and real for airlines.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/surveillance.png){width="100%"} 

<!-- {fig-cap="Deleted Surveillance Pricing Tweet. Source: https://www.instagram.com/p/DXmmV6GDbMx/"} -->
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](images/kalesalad-thread.png){width="100%"} 

<!-- {fig-cap="Airline Pricing Thread. Source: https://www.threads.com/@kalesalad/post/DXKSVR5jkLt/you-cant-even-get-a-hamburger-for-anymore"} -->
:::

::::

Since the deleted tweet, a lawsuit has been filed against JetBlue for collecting consumer data without consent and using such data to set ticket prices, and others have taken to social media to express their thoughts and frustrations with the airline system in the United States [@cbs_jetblue_surveillance_pricing].


Buying plane tickets has always been more complicated than simply buying your groceries. Even before this scandal, there still existed tips and tricks on how to ‘game’ the system to get the cheapest airline tickets possible. Influencers on social media have entire brands that are built around this. 

![](images/instagram.PNG){width="25%" fig-align="center"} 


But, do flight prices truly vary so randomly? What does the data actually say? See the following visualizations below to learn about the relationship between flight fares and prices. 

This data visualization project uses data from the Bureau of Transportation Statistics (BTS) DB1B Market survey, covering US domestic airline routes from Q3 2024 to Q2 2025, with over 25 million ticketing records.


## Flight Fare by Distance and Flight Fare by Route

How strongly is your airline ticket price related to the distance of the flight? Unsurprisingly, longer flights tend to have higher median flight prices. But perhaps surprisingly, the median price of a flight that covers a distance of 100-500 miles is only slightly higher than the median price of a flight that covers the distance of 2300-4200 miles. 

![](images/static_viz_1.png)

What about your flight fare and the airlines you fly out of and into. Looking at the top 10 most popular routes, we see that depending on the route you fly, your airline ticket can vary vastly. For example, if you fly from the San Francisco International Airport (SFO) to the San Diego International Airport (SAN), you could expect the price of your flight to fall within a relatively narrow range. But, if you fly from John F Kennedy International Airport (JFK) in New York to Los Angeles International Airport (LAX), the price of your flight could be as cheap as $250, but also as expensive as almost $2000. 


![](images/static_viz_2.png)


## What are the most popular flight paths?

The map below shows the top 500 busiest US domestic routes. Line color indicates average fare (darker blue = more expensive) and line thickness reflects total passengers. Hover over any route for details.

<iframe
  src="images/interactive_viz_1.html"
  width="100%"
  height="600"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>


## Fewer Carriers, Higher Fares

When fewer airlines compete at an airport, fares tend to be higher. Each dot below is a US airport, sized by passenger volume. The dashed trendline and correlation coefficient confirm the relationship: less competition means higher prices.

<iframe
  src="images/interactive_viz_2.html"
  width="100%"
  height="520"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>

## Airport Market Structure: Linked View

The map below shows US airports colored by number of carriers. Click any airport to highlight its position in the scatterplot on the right, which shows HHI (a measure of market concentration) against average fare. Higher HHI means less competition.

<iframe
  src="images/linked_view.html"
  width="100%"
  height="500"
  style="border: none;">
</iframe>

## Infographic

Double marginalization occurs when a manufacturer and a retailer both mark up the price of a product, leading to higher prices for consumers. In the airline industry, this can happen when airlines sell tickets through a third-party flight operator that also take a commission/contact fee. The infographic below illustrates this concept in the context of airline pricing.

![Airline Pricing Infographic](images/infographic.png)

## Flight Price & Route Explorer

To view the Flight Price & Route Explorer, click the button below. 

[View Interactive Map](https://5200-data-viz.streamlit.app/){.btn .btn-primary target="_blank"}
